reasoning.issue: The question asks what test the Cumulative Amount (build-up/available amount) basket is based on and when it begins to accrue.
reasoning.analysis: The Cumulative Amount is a build-up basket composed of multiple additive sources, with the core earnings-based component determined by a 'greatest of' three-test structure. The three-test aggregate (norm 6e76ed06_builder_source_three_test_aggregate) takes the greatest of: (1) 50% of cumulative Consolidated Net Income since the start anchor (6e76ed06_builder_source_cni); (2) the Available Retained ECF Amount — i.e., cumulative Excess Cash Flow not required to prepay term loans under Section 2.10(f) (6e76ed06_builder_source_ecf); and (3) 140% of LTM Consolidated EBITDA (6e76ed06_builder_source_ebitda_fc). This greatest-of result is then summed (aggregation_function: sum) with additional standalone sources: (a) a starter amount equal to the greater of $130,000,000 and 100% of LTM Consolidated EBITDA (6e76ed06_builder_source_starter); (b) 100% of equity proceeds not otherwise applied (6e76ed06_builder_source_other_equity_proceeds); (c) retained net cash proceeds from asset sales (6e76ed06_builder_source_retained_asset_sale_proceeds); (d) investment returns/dividends received (6e76ed06_builder_source_investment_returns); and (e) amounts from debt-to-equity conversions (6e76ed06_builder_source_other_debt_conversion). All sources share the same growth_start_anchor: 'closing_date_fiscal_quarter_start', confirmed by the start_date_language attribute on the builder_basket entity: 'the first day of the fiscal quarter in which the Closing Date occurs'. The basket is reduced by RP usage, RDP usage, and investment usage (reduced_by_rp_usage, reduced_by_rdp_usage, reduced_by_investment_usage all = true), but is NOT reduced by unrestricted subsidiary designations (reduced_by_designation = false).
reasoning.interactions: The builder basket (6e76ed06_builder_usage_permission) is a general_purpose capacity basket that shares its pool with the builder RDP basket (6e76ed06_builder_rdp_basket_permission / 6e76ed06_builder_source_three_test_aggregate), as confirmed by shares_with_rp_builder = true on the builder_rdp_basket entity. Draws under Section 6.09(a)(A) (restricted debt payments) therefore reduce the same Cumulative Amount available for restricted payments and investments under Section 6.03(x). The proceeds flow from asset sale events feeds into 6e76ed06_builder_source_retained_asset_sale_proceeds for the portion of net cash proceeds retained after the leverage-based sweep tiers.
reasoning.conclusion: The Cumulative Amount basket is based on a 'greatest of' three-test structure (50% CNI vs. retained ECF vs. 140% LTM EBITDA), plus additive starter, equity proceeds, asset sale proceeds, investment returns, and debt conversion sources, and begins accruing on the first day of the fiscal quarter in which the Closing Date occurs.

stage2.answer: The Cumulative Amount (build-up basket) is defined at the 'Definition of Cumulative Amount' (p. 32) and is based on the sum (without duplication) of several additive sources, the core of which is the **greatest of**: (i) 50% of cumulative Consolidated Net Income, (ii) the Available Retained ECF Amount (cumulative Excess Cash Flow not required to prepay term loans under Section 2.10(f)), and (iii) 140% of LTM Consolidated EBITDA — with that greatest-of result then added to a starter amount equal to the greater of $130,000,000 and 100% of LTM Consolidated EBITDA, plus 100% of equity proceeds not otherwise applied, retained asset sale proceeds, investment returns, and debt-to-equity conversion amounts [Definition of Cumulative Amount, p. 32]. The basket begins growing on **the first day of the fiscal quarter in which the Closing Date occurs** [Definition of Cumulative Amount, p. 32; builder_basket entity attr: start_date_language]. The Cumulative Amount is reduced dollar-for-dollar by usage under the RP covenant, the restricted debt payment covenant (Section 6.09(a)(A)), and the investment covenant (Section 6.03(x)), but is not reduced by unrestricted subsidiary designations. The builder basket shares its pool with the builder RDP basket under Section 6.09(a)(A) [Section 6.09(a)(A), p. 199].

questions.gold_answer: The Cumulative Amount is based on the greatest of three tests: (1) 50% of cumulative Consolidated Net Income (which amount shall not be less than zero for any fiscal quarter), (2) Excess Cash Flow not required to be applied to prepay Term Loans or any other debt (such amount cannot be less than $0 for any fiscal year), (3) cumulative Consolidated EBITDA minus 140% of cumulative Consolidated Fixed Charges. All tests start growing from the first day of the fiscal quarter in which the Closing Date occurs.

reasoning.issue: Whether the Borrower is permitted to distribute (by dividend or otherwise) the equity interests it holds in Unrestricted Subsidiaries, and on what basis.
reasoning.analysis: Section 6.06(p) contains a categorical, express permission allowing the distribution 'by dividend or otherwise, of shares of Equity Interests of, or Indebtedness owed to Holdings or a Restricted Subsidiary by, or any assets of, an Unrestricted Subsidiary or the proceeds thereof.' The extracted norm (6e76ed06_unrestricted_sub_equity_distribution_permission) has modality 'permission', is_categorical = true, and partial_applicability = false, meaning it applies without limitation or condition. The covered objects include equity interests (covers_equity_interests: true), indebtedness (covers_indebtedness: true), assets (covers_assets: true), and proceeds (covers_proceeds: true). No defeaters are listed against this norm. The requires_valid_designation flag is false, meaning the Borrower need not satisfy any additional designation prerequisite at the time of the distribution. The basket also includes noncash distributions at FMV (includes_noncash_at_fmv: true) and distributions can cascade through the structure (distributions_can_cascade: true). Notably, the capacity_category is 'categorical', meaning there is no dollar cap or financial ratio test attached to this specific permission. The basket rebuilds the builder basket (rebuilds_builder_basket: true), so distributions made under it do not reduce the Cumulative Amount. No anti-duplication provision applies (has_anti_duplication: false). The general RP basket (6.06(j)) and ratio basket (6.06(o)) provide additional independent avenues but are not needed given the categorical nature of 6.06(p).
reasoning.interactions: No defeaters override norm 6e76ed06_unrestricted_sub_equity_distribution_permission. The unsub_distribution_basket does not reduce the investment basket and rebuilds the builder basket, so using it does not impair other RP capacity. The J.Crew blocker (jcrew_blocker entity) applies only at the designation stage (covers_designation: true; covers_transfer: false), not to subsequent distributions of equity already held in an Unrestricted Subsidiary. No proceeds-flow mechanics are triggered by an equity dividend of Unrestricted Subsidiary interests.
reasoning.conclusion: Yes — Section 6.06(p) provides a categorical, uncapped, unconditioned permission for the Borrower to distribute by dividend the equity interests (and other assets or indebtedness) of an Unrestricted Subsidiary, with no dollar cap, no ratio test, and no requirement that the subsidiary have been validly designated at the time of distribution.

stage2.answer: Yes. Section 6.06(p) [p. 198] expressly and categorically permits the Borrower to distribute 'by dividend or otherwise, shares of Equity Interests of … an Unrestricted Subsidiary or the proceeds thereof' without any dollar cap, financial ratio condition, or pro forma compliance requirement. The permission also covers indebtedness owed by the Unrestricted Subsidiary and its assets, and applies to non-cash distributions at fair market value. No defeaters or carve-outs override this permission, and using it rebuilds (rather than reduces) the Cumulative Amount builder basket.

reasoning.issue: The question asks whether any investment, restricted debt prepayment (RDP), or other cross-covenant baskets can be reallocated and used to make restricted payments or dividends.
reasoning.analysis:
Three reallocation pathways are relevant:

1. GENERAL RDP BASKET → RP: The general RDP basket [Section 6.09(a)(I)] permits prepayments up to the greater of $130M / 100% Consolidated EBITDA. Its action_scope is 'reallocable' and it is classified as general_purpose with additive composition. Separately, the general RP basket [Section 6.06(j)] is also the greater of $130M / 100% Consolidated EBITDA and is marked 'fungible' and 'reallocable'. The data shows these are independent general_purpose baskets (no shares_capacity_pool link between them), meaning the general RDP basket capacity is available for RDPs but is not itself directly reallocated into RP capacity; rather, both draw from independent dollar pools.

2. CUMULATIVE AMOUNT (BUILDER) — SHARED POOL: The builder basket [Definition of Cumulative Amount, p.32] is the most important reallocation mechanism. The builder_rdp_basket [Section 6.09(a)(A)] explicitly 'shares_with_rp_builder': true — meaning the Cumulative Amount is a shared pool that is reduced by RP usage, RDP usage, and investment usage (reduced_by_rp_usage=true, reduced_by_rdp_usage=true, reduced_by_investment_usage=true). This means any unused Cumulative Amount capacity can be used for RPs, RDPs, or investments interchangeably, but all three draw from the same pool (no double-counting). The builder basket also accepts investment returns, retained asset sale proceeds, ECF, equity proceeds, and debt conversion proceeds as sources.

3. INVESTMENT BASKET [Section 6.03(y)]: The general investment basket under Section 6.03(y) is sized as the greater of $130M/100% EBITDA PLUS the aggregate total of all amounts available under the RDP basket (Section 6.09(a)). This means RDP capacity feeds into investment basket capacity — capacity flows from RDP → investments, but the data does not show a direct path from the investment basket back to RP. Investment pathway 6.03(x) uses the Cumulative Amount directly, linking investments and RPs through that shared pool.

4. RATIO BASKET: The ratio RDP basket [Section 6.09(a)(J)] permits unlimited RDPs if First Lien Leverage Ratio ≤ 5.75x pro forma (with closing-ratio alternative). The ratio RP basket [Section 6.06(o)] permits unlimited dividends at the same 5.75x threshold (or no-worse test). These operate independently at the same leverage test but are not cross-reallocated — they are concurrent permissions at the same condition.
reasoning.interactions: The Cumulative Amount is the primary cross-covenant shared pool: it is reduced by RP usage, RDP usage, and investment usage alike (builder_basket attrs: reduced_by_rp_usage=true, reduced_by_rdp_usage=true, reduced_by_investment_usage=true; builder_rdp_basket: shares_with_rp_builder=true). The general investment basket under 6.03(y) is explicitly sized to include RDP basket availability as an additive component, meaning RDP capacity indirectly expands investment capacity. Asset sale proceeds not swept flow into the Cumulative Amount (proceeds_flow: retained_after_sweep → builder_source_retained_asset_sale_proceeds), further linking asset sale economics to RP/RDP/investment capacity.
reasoning.conclusion: Yes — the Cumulative Amount (builder basket) is a shared fungible pool that can be used interchangeably for restricted payments, restricted debt prepayments, and investments, and the general investment basket under Section 6.03(y) expressly incorporates RDP basket availability as an additive sizing component.

stage2.answer: Yes, there are meaningful reallocation pathways. First, and most significantly, the Cumulative Amount (builder basket) [Definition of Cumulative Amount, p.32] is a shared general-purpose pool that is drawn down equally by restricted payments, restricted debt prepayments, and investments — unused capacity in one use can be redirected to another, including dividends, subject to the pool not being exhausted [Section 6.09(a)(A), p.199; shares_with_rp_builder=true]. Second, the general investment basket under Section 6.03(y) is sized as the greater of $130M/100% EBITDA plus the aggregate amounts available under Section 6.09(a) (the RDP basket), meaning RDP capacity directly inflates the investment basket's cap, though this path flows RDP → investment rather than investment → RP. Third, the general RP basket [Section 6.06(j), p.196] and general RDP basket [Section 6.09(a)(I), p.200] are each independently sized at the greater of $130M/100% EBITDA and both carry 'reallocable' action_scope, but absent an explicit shares_capacity_pool link in the data, they appear to operate as separate pools rather than a single shared pool. The ratio baskets for both RPs [Section 6.06(o), p.198] and RDPs [Section 6.09(a)(J), p.200] provide unlimited capacity at the same First Lien Leverage Ratio threshold (≤5.75x pro forma) but are concurrent independent permissions rather than cross-reallocated pools.

questions.gold_answer: Yes, under 6.06(j) amount available for Restricted Debt Payment under 6.09(a) and amounts available for Investments under 6.03(y) can be reallocated to the making of Dividends. 6.09(a) includes the greater of $130,000,000 and 100% of Consolidated EBITDA. 6.09(a) also includes other more tailored baskets available for Restricted Debt Payments which may or may not be available for reallocation, including intercompany debt payments and payments in connection with a reorganization or IPO.

reasoning.issue: The question asks whether asset sale proceeds can be used to fund dividend payments under the restricted payments covenant.
reasoning.analysis: There are two distinct pathways by which asset sale proceeds can fund dividends. First, and most directly, the Cumulative Amount (builder basket) expressly includes a source for retained asset sale proceeds (norm 6e76ed06_builder_source_retained_asset_sale_proceeds), which contributes additively to the builder basket usage permission (6e76ed06_builder_usage_permission). Per the proceeds_flow entry, Net Cash Proceeds from asset sales flow into clause (f) of the Cumulative Amount to the extent they are retained after application of the leverage-based mandatory sweep under Section 2.10(c). The sweep tiers show: 100% of proceeds must prepay term loans if First Lien Leverage Ratio > 5.75x; 50% if between 5.50x and 5.75x; and 0% (no sweep) if ≤ 5.50x. Only the portion not required to be swept enters the Cumulative Amount. Once in the Cumulative Amount, those proceeds can be used for dividends under 6e76ed06_builder_usage_permission. Second, the general RP basket (6e76ed06_general_rp_basket_permission) permits dividends up to the greater of $130M or 100% of Consolidated EBITDA without specifying a funding source restriction — so asset sale proceeds could fund dividends up to that cap without being earmarked. Third, the ratio basket (6e76ed06_ratio_rp_basket_permission) permits unlimited dividends if First Lien Leverage Ratio ≤ 5.75x (or no worse than pre-transaction), also without funding source restrictions. Fourth, the unrestricted subsidiary distribution basket (6e76ed06_unrestricted_sub_equity_distribution_permission, Section 6.06(p)) expressly permits distribution of 'the proceeds' of assets of an Unrestricted Subsidiary, which could include proceeds from asset sales at the unrestricted subsidiary level.
reasoning.interactions: The retained asset sale proceeds source (6e76ed06_builder_source_retained_asset_sale_proceeds) feeds into the builder basket (6e76ed06_builder_usage_permission) via a sum aggregation at child_index 6. The amount available is reduced by the mandatory sweep — only proceeds not swept under Section 2.10(c) (based on the three leverage tiers) enter the builder basket. The builder basket capacity is also reduced by prior RP, RDP, and investment usage. The general RP basket and ratio basket are independent and have no source-of-funds restriction. No defeaters override any of these norms.
reasoning.conclusion: Yes, asset sale proceeds can be used to make dividends through multiple pathways: (1) retained asset sale proceeds (after any mandatory sweep) build Cumulative Amount capacity usable for dividends; (2) the general RP basket and ratio basket permit dividends without funding source restrictions; and (3) proceeds of Unrestricted Subsidiary assets may be distributed as dividends categorically under Section 6.06(p).

stage2.answer: Yes, asset sale proceeds can be used to make dividends through at least three pathways. First, Net Cash Proceeds from asset sales that are not required to be swept under the mandatory prepayment tiers (Section 2.10(c)) flow into the Cumulative Amount as retained asset sale proceeds, which the builder basket then makes available for dividends [Definition of Cumulative Amount, p. 32; sweep tiers at Definition of Applicable Net Cash Proceeds Percentage]. The sweep is 100% if First Lien Leverage Ratio > 5.75x, 50% if between 5.50x–5.75x, and 0% if ≤ 5.50x — so at or below 5.50x leverage, all asset sale proceeds are retained and may fuel the builder basket. Second, the general RP basket permits dividends up to the greater of $130,000,000 or 100% of Consolidated EBITDA without any restriction on the source of funding [Section 6.06(j), p. 196], so asset sale proceeds could fund dividends within that cap. Third, the ratio basket permits unlimited dividends on a pro forma basis if First Lien Leverage Ratio is ≤ 5.75x (or no worse than pre-transaction) [Section 6.06(o), p. 198], also with no source-of-funds restriction. Additionally, proceeds of assets of an Unrestricted Subsidiary may be distributed as dividends on a categorical basis without dollar limit [Section 6.06(p), p. 198].

questions.gold_answer: Yes, Retained Asset Sale Proceeds build the Cumulative Amount which consists of proceeds from: Net Cash Proceeds from asset sales not subject to prepayment on account of Section 2.10(c)(iv), permitting proceeds from any Asset Sale using the unlimited basket 6.05(z) if such Asset Sale is a sale of a product line and the pro forma First Lien Net Leverage Ratio is 6.25x or less or if such test is no worse pro forma. Also includes asset sale proceeds not swept when First Lien Net Leverage Ratio is 5.75x or less (50% of proceeds) or 5.50x or less (100% of proceeds). Also includes Net Cash Proceeds from non-collateral assets, ordinary course asset sales, asset sales from non-ratio baskets, casualty events, and proceeds from collateral assets below de minimis thresholds of $20M/15% EBITDA individual and $40M/30% EBITDA annual.

Step 2 — Sum general_purpose baskets. Two general_purpose RP baskets exist: (1) the general RP basket at $130M / 100% EBITDA (Section 6.06(j)) and (2) the builder basket (Cumulative Amount). The general_rp_basket has capacity_composition = fungible; the builder_basket has capacity_composition = computed_from_sources. No shares_capacity_pool link between them is indicated, so they are independent pools. The general RDP basket (Section 6.09(a)(I), $130M / 100% EBITDA) and builder RDP basket (Section 6.09(a)(A)) are RDP baskets, not RP baskets for dividends — they are part of the restricted debt payment covenant hierarchy, not the dividend-capacity total.

reasoning.issue: Whether the Borrower can dividend a $200m asset/business division (with negative EBITDA) to shareholders when the First Lien Net Leverage Ratio is 6.0x, focusing on which RP baskets are available.
reasoning.analysis:
Three baskets are potentially relevant:

1. RATIO BASKET (Section 6.06(o), norm 6e76ed06_ratio_rp_basket_permission): Permits unlimited dividends (including of a business_division per the scope_edges) on a Pro Forma Basis if the First Lien Leverage Ratio is no greater than the GREATER OF (x) 5.75x or (y) the ratio WITHOUT giving effect to the transaction (no-worse test). At a current ratio of 6.0x pre-transaction, condition (x) fails (6.0x > 5.75x). However, the 'no-worse test' (condition_root_1, predicate: pro_forma_no_worse|||first_lien_net_leverage) must be examined. The asset being dividended has NEGATIVE EBITDA. Removing a negative-EBITDA asset from the consolidated group on a pro forma basis INCREASES consolidated EBITDA (the denominator increases), which DECREASES the First Lien Leverage Ratio. Therefore, the pro forma ratio after the dividend will be LOWER than the pre-transaction ratio of 6.0x. The no-worse test asks whether the pro forma ratio is no greater than the ratio WITHOUT giving effect to the transaction (i.e., 6.0x). Since removing negative EBITDA improves leverage, the pro forma ratio will be ≤ 6.0x — the no-worse test IS SATISFIED. The condition tree is an OR, so satisfying either branch is sufficient. The ratio basket is therefore AVAILABLE.

2. GENERAL BASKET (Section 6.06(j), norm 6e76ed06_general_rp_basket_permission): Permits dividends up to the greater of $130m or 100% of Consolidated EBITDA, unconditioned on leverage. However, this basket covers 'equity_interest' as the object class per its v3 attrs but the scope of the general_rp_basket has partial_applicability=false, and the source_text references making dividends 'to Holdings and/or Holdings' direct or indirect equity holders.' This basket is available as a fallback up to its cap ($130m or 100% EBITDA), but $200m in assets likely exceeds this cap unless EBITDA is sufficiently large, and the basket measures aggregate dollar usage, not FMV of distributed assets. Its availability is subject to available capacity.

3. UNSUB DISTRIBUTION BASKET (Section 6.06(p)): Only applies if the asset/division is held by an Unrestricted Subsidiary — not directly applicable here unless the division is first dropped into an Unrestricted Sub (which would require a separate investment using available capacity, and the unsub_designation has a $40m cap per the designation entity).

Conclusion: The ratio basket under 6.06(o) is the key available pathway via the no-worse test, because dividending a negative-EBITDA business improves the pro forma leverage ratio below the pre-transaction 6.0x level.

Since the dividend removes negative EBITDA, the pro forma ratio improves below 6.0x, satisfying condition (2). The general basket (6.06(j)) is independent capacity that could cover smaller distributions without a leverage test. No defeaters are present in the data.
reasoning.conclusion: The Borrower CAN dividend the $200m negative-EBITDA asset/business division to shareholders at a 6.0x First Lien Net Leverage Ratio, because the no-worse test prong of the ratio basket under Section 6.06(o) is satisfied: removing a negative-EBITDA asset improves the pro forma leverage ratio below the pre-transaction 6.0x level, meeting the OR condition.

stage2.answer: Yes, the Borrower can dividend the $200m asset/business division at a 6.0x First Lien Net Leverage Ratio, primarily through the ratio basket under Section 6.06(o) [p. 198]. While the absolute 5.75x threshold is not met (6.0x > 5.75x), Section 6.06(o) contains an OR condition: the basket is also available if the pro forma First Lien Leverage Ratio (after giving effect to the dividend) is no greater than the ratio WITHOUT giving effect to the transaction (i.e., the 'no-worse test'). Because the business division has negative EBITDA, dividending it removes negative EBITDA from the consolidated denominator, which INCREASES consolidated EBITDA and thereby DECREASES the pro forma leverage ratio below 6.0x — satisfying the no-worse test. As a fallback, the general basket under Section 6.06(j) [p. 196] provides additional capacity up to the greater of $130m or 100% of Consolidated EBITDA without any leverage condition, though this may not cover the full $200m value depending on available capacity and how the distribution is measured.
